title: Tax brackets and the marginal rate
deck: Moving into a higher bracket does not mean all your income gets taxed at the higher rate. Only the dollars above the bracket threshold do. The math behind one of the most-misunderstood ideas in personal finance.
objectives: Apply the U.S. progressive income-tax system: identify the marginal tax rate for a given taxable income, distinguish marginal from effective rate, and compute the total tax owed across multiple brackets.

content.text: A co-worker is offered a raise from $46,000 to $48,000 and frets: “That will push me into the 22% bracket. I might end up with less take-home pay than before.” It is one of the most widespread misconceptions in personal finance, repeated in family dinners and break-room conversations across the country — and it is mathematically wrong.

The U.S. federal income tax is a progressive tax. Each successive slice of income is taxed at its own rate, and moving into a higher bracket affects only the dollars above that bracket's threshold. The dollars below the threshold continue to be taxed exactly as they were. A raise from $46,000 to $48,000 cannot reduce take-home pay; the new $2,000 is taxed at a higher rate than the old earnings, but the old earnings are unaffected. This section develops the bracket math precisely, distinguishes the marginal rate (the rate of the top bracket reached) from the effective rate (total tax divided by total income), and shows why the effective rate is always less than the marginal rate.

The U.S. federal income tax divides each filer's taxable income into slices by bracket thresholds and taxes each slice at its own rate. For a single filer in 2024 the brackets are approximately as follows: 10% on the first $11,600 of taxable income; 12% from $11,600 to $47,150; 22% from $47,150 to $100,525; 24% from $100,525 to $191,950; 32% from $191,950 to $243,725; 35% from $243,725 to $609,350; and 37% above $609,350. (The thresholds are roughly doubled for married-filing-jointly returns and adjusted annually for inflation; the rate schedule has stayed largely fixed since the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act.)

content.caption: Figure 7.2. The bracket staircase. A $60,000 taxable income is split across three brackets: $11,600 taxed at 10%, $35,550 taxed at 12%, and the remaining $12,850 taxed at 22%. The total tax owed is the sum of the three slices.

content.text: Definition 7.2. The marginal tax rate is the rate of the highest bracket the taxpayer's income reaches — equivalently, the rate at which the next dollar earned would be taxed. The effective tax rate is total tax owed divided by total taxable income. In a progressive system, the effective rate is always less than (or equal to) the marginal rate.

Computing total tax for a given taxable income is a stair-step calculation: tax each bracket-width slice at that bracket's rate, then sum the results. For an income of $60,000 (still using the 2024 single-filer brackets): the first $11,600 is taxed at 10%, producing $1,160 of tax; the next $35,550 (from $11,600 to $47,150) is taxed at 12%, producing $4,266 of tax; the final $12,850 (from $47,150 to $60,000) is taxed at 22%, producing $2,827 of tax. The total is 1,160 + 4,266 + 2,827 = $8,253. The marginal rate is 22% (the top bracket reached). The effective rate is 8,253 / 60,000 ≈ 13.76%, well below the headline “22% bracket” figure.
The marginal rate is what the next dollar costs. The effective rate is what every dollar so far has cost on average. The two are always different in a progressive system.

Margin noteState income tax (in most states) layers on top of the federal calculation, often with its own progressive bracket structure. Combining federal + state + FICA, the total marginal rate for a typical middle-class earner is often in the 25%-35% range — the cumulative tax cost of the next dollar earned. Tax-deferred retirement accounts (401(k)s, traditional IRAs) shield contributions from this combined marginal rate, deferring tax until retirement when the worker is often in a lower bracket.

Common mistakeApplying a single rate to the entire income. Multiplying $60,000 by 22% gives $13,200, which is what you would owe if the U.S. used a flat tax. The actual progressive calculation gives $8,253 — almost $5,000 less. Always stair-step across the brackets rather than applying a single rate.

Example 7.2. Computing tax on $60,000 of taxable income.

A single filer reports $60,000 of taxable income. Using the 2024 brackets (10% to $11,600; 12% to $47,150; 22% to $100,525), compute the total federal income tax owed, the marginal rate, and the effective rate.

Solution.

Step 1. Identify the top bracket reached. $60,000 falls in the 22% bracket, so the marginal rate is 22%. Three brackets are touched.

Step 2. Stair-step the tax: 10% × $11,600 = $1,160; 12% × ($47,150 − $11,600) = 12% × $35,550 = $4,266; 22% × ($60,000 − $47,150) = 22% × $12,850 = $2,827.

total tax = $1,160 + $4,266 + $2,827 = $8,253

Step 3. Effective rate = $8,253 / $60,000 ≈ 0.1376 = 13.76%.

The taxpayer's headline bracket (22%) and their actual rate of taxation (13.76%) are very different numbers. The headline rate matters for decisions about whether to earn more; the effective rate matters for understanding the overall tax burden.

In the wildThe current U.S. bracket structure has seven brackets and dates in roughly its current form to the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, with annual inflation adjustments to the thresholds. Pre-2017, the top bracket was 39.6%; the 2017 law reduced it to 37%. The progressive bracket system itself dates to the Sixteenth Amendment to the Constitution (1913), which authorized the federal income tax. Before 1913, the federal government raised most of its revenue from tariffs and excise taxes, and there was no individual income tax in peacetime.

1. A single filer has $40,000 of taxable income. Using the 2024 brackets (10% to $11,600; 12% to $47,150), compute the total federal income tax owed.

    Show answer

    Answer. $4,568. 10% × 11,600 = $1,160; 12% × (40,000 − 11,600) = 12% × 28,400 = $3,408; total $4,568.

  2. What is the marginal rate for a single filer with $75,000 of taxable income (using the 2024 brackets)?

    Show answer

    Answer. 22%. $75,000 falls inside the 22% bracket (which runs from $47,150 to $100,525), so the rate of the highest bracket reached is 22%.

  3. True or false: if a raise pushes you into a higher tax bracket, all of your income is taxed at the new higher rate.

    Show answer

    Answer. False. Only the income above the bracket threshold is taxed at the new rate; the lower slices continue to be taxed at their original rates. A raise can never reduce take-home pay through bracket effects alone.

  4. For the single filer with $60,000 of taxable income from Example 7.2, by how much does the effective rate differ from the marginal rate?

    Show answer

    Answer. 8.24 percentage points. The effective rate is 13.76% and the marginal rate is 22%, a difference of 22 − 13.76 = 8.24 percentage points.

  5. Compute the total federal income tax for a single filer with $30,000 of taxable income.

    Show answer

    Answer. $3,368. 10% × $11,600 = $1,160; 12% × (30,000 − 11,600) = 12% × $18,400 = $2,208; total $3,368.

  6. Which is the correct relationship between the marginal rate and the effective rate in a progressive system?

    1. Marginal < effective always
    2. Effective < marginal always
    3. The two are equal
    4. No relationship
    Show answer

    Answer. B. The effective rate is always less than the marginal rate in a progressive system (with equality only when income lies entirely within the first bracket).

content.title: Bracket-by-bracket tax calculation
content.problem: Using the 2024 single-filer brackets, compute the **federal income tax** on a **taxable income of $60,000** (after the standard deduction).
content.solution:
**Walk the brackets bottom-up.**

2024 single-filer rates:

• 10% on income from $0 to $11,600

• 12% on income from $11,600 to $47,150

• 22% on income from $47,150 to $100,525

Income breakdown:

• First $11,600: tax = 11,600 × 0.10 = $1,160.

• Next $35,550 (from $11,600 to $47,150): tax = 35,550 × 0.12 = $4,266.

• Final $12,850 (from $47,150 to $60,000): tax = 12,850 × 0.22 = $2,827.

**Total federal tax = 1,160 + 4,266 + 2,827 = $8,253.**

*Sanity check.* That's about 13.8% of $60,000 — the **effective rate**, even though the *marginal* rate (the rate on the next dollar earned) is 22%.

content.title: Marginal vs effective rate (and why it matters)
content.problem: For the **$60,000** taxable income from Example 7.2.1: what is the **marginal** rate, what is the **effective** rate, and why does the difference matter for decisions like asking for a raise?
content.solution:
**Marginal rate** = the rate on your *next* dollar of income.

Your $60,000 income sits in the 22% bracket (the bracket spans $47,150 to $100,525). **Marginal rate = 22%**.

**Effective rate** = total tax / taxable income × 100.

From Example 7.2.1, total tax = $8,253.

Effective rate = 8,253 / 60,000 × 100 ≈ **13.8%**.

*Why the difference matters.* If you get a **$3,000 raise**, *all* of that raise is taxed at the **marginal** rate (22%), not the effective rate. So you'd keep $3,000 × (1 − 0.22) = **$2,340** of the raise.

But your average tax rate barely moves — the new effective rate is (8,253 + 660) / 63,000 ≈ 14.1%. So the headline "you'll be paying 22%" is misleading: only *the raise itself* is taxed at 22%; the rest of your income is still taxed by the lower-bracket schedule. **Raises always increase your take-home — even when they push you into a higher bracket.**

content.title: Summary
content.content: Federal income tax is computed bracket by bracket, not at one flat rate. The **marginal rate** applies to your next dollar of income; the **effective rate** is total tax divided by taxable income (always lower than the marginal rate). Understanding this distinction is what separates people who fear raises from people who actually take them.

content.text: The bracket math determines what an individual filer owes for the year. Section 7.3 closes the income-tax half of the chapter by reconciling that owed amount against the withholding accumulated across all the year's paychecks, settling up on April 15 with either a refund or an additional payment.
